frozen_components: Qwen2.5-72B-Base and Qwen2.5-7B-Base (generation and entropy computation)
architecture.summary: EDU-PRM is a process reward model trained on automatically-segmented reasoning steps. The system uses token-level predictive entropy (softmax over logits at each decoding step) to identify uncertainty anchors—positions where entropy exceeds a threshold—and branches into top-k continuations, then greedily decodes between anchors. Each resulting fragment is assigned a binary correctness label via Monte Carlo estimation over final-answer validity. The trained PRM then scores intermediate reasoning steps during inference, either for per-step evaluation (ProcessBench) or as a selector in Best-of-N candidate ranking.
trained_components: Process Reward Model (classification head on frozen base model)
Soft labels via Monte Carlo aggregation

biblio.title: More Bang for the Buck: Process Reward Modeling with Entropy-Driven Uncertainty

claims.statement: On the MATH dataset, EDU-PRM-72B attains the highest judgment accuracy, outperforming Qwen-2.5-math-PRM-72B by a margin of 1.5 percentage points.
EDU-PRM achieves comparable results to SOTA Qwen2.5-Math-PRM while using only 1.5% of its publicly reported process-level training data.
By leveraging EDU sampling strategy, accuracy boosts from 64.7% to 67.3% for generative reasoning tasks, accompanied by a reduction of 32% in token usage.
On MATH, EDU sampling achieves 71.2% accuracy with 1680 tokens per sample, while high-temperature sampling achieves 68.4% accuracy with 2340 tokens on average.
On OLY, EDU sampling attains 47.6% accuracy with 2340 tokens, compared to 42.8% of high-temperature sampling with 3480 tokens.

EDU-PRM automatically anchors step boundaries at tokens with high predictive entropy, effectively capturing intrinsic logical transitions and facilitating efficient exploration of diverse reasoning paths, without requiring manual step annotations.
Fragment-level rewards are inferred automatically via Monte Carlo aggregation based solely on final-answer correctness, without any step-wise human or LLM labeling.

The computational cost of entropy calculation during inference adds a slight overhead compared to standard greedy decoding; experiments focus primarily on mathematical reasoning with unclear generalizability to other domains such as coding or creative writing.
